issue_title: pycoin timings using pure Python code only, which is much slower than OpenSSL version
issue_body:
I'm the author of pycoin.

The page https://github.com/ofek/bit/blob/master/docs/source/guide/intro.rst show shows pycoin as being very slow generating sec blobs. This can be sped up very significantly by setting the environment variable ```PYCOIN_NATIVE=openssl``` which will use OpenSSL for two slow functions. See also the "Too slow?" section at https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/master/README.md
Only applicable in non-blocking asynchronous contexts, such as `aiohttp` client.

When your bot gets kicked from the server for various reasons like "Excess flood" and other things and then reconnects. It does not call on_welcome again. Is this a bug or it is meant to be this way?

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/9
- Originally reported by: [unconscious](http://bitbucket.org/unconscious)
- Originally created at: 2012-12-08T14:21:11.332

Hello. I've written a bot using your very nice IRC library. Up until now, there have no been issues. Today a  german used the bot w/xchat on windows and the library throws an exception while decoding lines with umlauts and ß and other unicode chars. 

Here's a traceback:
```
  File "/usr/local/bin/hanabIRC", line 143, in <module>
    bot.start()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/bot.py", line 266, in start
    super(SingleServerIRCBot, self).start()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/client.py", line 1263, in start
    self.manifold.process_forever()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/client.py", line 280, in process_forever
    self.process_once(timeout)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/client.py", line 261, in process_once
    self.process_data(i)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/client.py", line 218, in process_data
    c.process_data()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/client.py", line 575, in process_data
    for line in self.buffer:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/buffer.py", line 94, in lines
    self.handle_exception()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/irc/buffer.py", line 92, in lines
    yield line.decode(self.encoding, self.errors)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/encodings/utf_8.py", line 16, in decode
    return codecs.utf_8_decode(input, errors, True)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xdf in position 77: invalid continuation byte
```

- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/40
- Originally reported by: Phil Stein
- Originally created at: 2014-06-05T05:06:05.310

issue_title: Emit a signal when the token is refreshed.
issue_body:
- [ ] Emit a signal when the refresh() is triggered (meaning access_token is expired) (we'll probably need a default handler) 
- [ ] Allow the user to override the called method when the signal is emitted.

It's currently not possible to reliably check whether a JSON response is an empty object:

```yaml
response_strings:
- "{}"
```

seems to be the closest we can get, but could easily result in false positives for nested objects.

Even negation (which isn't supported by JSONPath anyway, AFAIK) wouldn't help, as there might not be a fixed list of keys to blacklist.

Thus it seems like we need some sort of `response_body` assertion, comparing against the entire body? Of course that'll inevitably raise the question of leading/trailing whitespace...
Once 1.0.0 is released, set up docs so that stable points to the release version.

issue_title: Viterbi 算法得到的是类似贪心算法的局部最优？
issue_body:
Wiki 上的 Python 算法针对的是又两个隐藏状态的特例，所以可以像贪心一样从 起点开始，不断向前选择，每次选择都没有后效性。

现在对于分词的问题，有四个隐藏状态，那么这个时候的 Viterbi 算法 要得到全局最优解应该只能从从后往前回溯得到。如果从前往后，每一步选择局部最优，得到的结果不是全局最优。

英文维基上的伪代码是全局最优的一个通用实现，Python 的不是。

在维基参考文献上看到的一个比较清晰的[介绍](http://www.kanungo.com/software/hmmtut.pdf) 24页。

![Viterbi 算法截图](http://wertion.github.io/pic/hmmtut_%E9%A1%B5%E9%9D%A2_24.png) 

我的一些理解哈，也可能不对，麻烦大家看一下吧。
在统计词与邻近词的连通数的时候，为什么只统计后面的词，按道理说前后的词都要统计的吧？

issue_title: Fail if a configured linter is not found
issue_body:
If a linter is listed in the configuration (eg: isort) pylama should fail if the linter is not found instead of giving only a warning: https://github.com/klen/pylama/blob/4e1994af951266467585b4fead84ecd5afca5c53/pylama/config.py#L68

This could be implemented as a setting to enforce or not enforce.

issue_title: Prefer is# and is? instead of ==# and ==?
issue_body:
When you compare expressions with String literals, Vint will tell you to use `==#` or `==?` instead of `==`, as `==` changes depending on user configuration, and Vint will do the same with `is` and strings, preferring `is#` and `is?`. I think Vint should go one step further and disallow `==#` or `==?` for String literal comparisons too, and prefer `is#` or `is?` instead.

If you compare `0` with any string which does not contain numbers using `==#` or `==?`, The comparison will return `1`. This is because with something like `0 ==# 'foo'`, Vim converts the RHS to a Number, and that conversion yield `0`, so the result is `1`. This is almost certainly not what you want. This has been a source of bugs for my project.

I would argue that if you're making a comparison between any value and a String, what you want is for that comparison to be true only if both sides of a comparison are String values. I think there's pretty much no reason to ever use `==#` or `==?`, and you should use `is#` or `is?` instead.

issue_title: Being able to do `async with async_thread()`
issue_body:
Just a thought/suggestion, not sure if you wish to add this in, but it feels like it would fit perfectly within curio's design: the ability to use `async_thread` inline as a context manager.

For example:
```py
await do_some_task_1()
async with async_thread():  # the body of the async with is now ran inside a thread
    r = do_some_blocking_operation_1()
    AWAIT(do_some_task_2(r))  # maybe even magic a regular await?
    r2 = do_some_blocking_operation(2)

print(r2)  # back in the curio thread
```

I know this is possible by yielding inside aenter or similar. This is inspired by asyncio-extra's ability to [the same thing](https://github.com/agronholm/asyncio_extras/blob/master/asyncio_extras/threads.py#L70).

issue_title: Better data structure for sleeping task queues
issue_body:
Right now, objects like mutexes hold sleeping tasks in a `deque`. This is nice because it supports O(1) FIFO operations. But removing an arbitrary task (e.g. due to cancellation or timeout) is O(n). Ideally our sleeping task queue should be O(1) for all of these operations.

I think probably the answer is to use an intrusive double-linked list. I.e., put `next` and `previous` pointers directly into the task object (a task can't sleep on multiple queues at once, so this is fine).

issue_title: Inconsistent ssl.py module with the same version number
Attach partially completed I/O to exceptions
issue_body:
There are several I/O functions such as `readall()` and `read_exactly()` that collect I/O into an internal buffer before returning it.   If cancelled, any partially read data is lost forever.   I'd like to attach it to the resulting exception object as an attribute.    The `sendall()` method of sockets already does this by attaching a `bytes_sent` attribute to the exception on cancellation.

Discovered this while looking at some of the code in `subprocess` which is supposed to return any I/O completed on timeout/cancellation (it's returned with the exception).

Hi,
When using Pyro4 with the default serpent serializer, I noticed that floats at the receiver side have a reduced 12-digit accuracy. For example:
```
SERPENT: 
1482514078.55
Original
1482514078.546359
```
It seems that in ser_builtins_float in serpent.py, a float is converted as follows:
`out.append(str(float_obj).encode("ascii"))`
while for full accuracy it should be like:
`out.append("{:f}".format(float_obj).encode("ascii"))`

Thanks for checking.

Edit:
> Python versions prior to 3.2 use 12 digits for the whole number when using str().
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/20586011/python-precision-in-string-formatting-with-float-numbers

issue_title: Colour output processor chokes on multi-part Actions

issue_title: Code snippet to support VeraSecure built in siren
issue_body:
Hi,
first off I'm a total noob on github so this is probably the wrong way to handle this. Secondly, big thanks for writing this library.

Now to the topic. 
I have a VeraSecure controller that is connected to Home Assistant that is using your library. The VeraSecure have an built in siren that is triggered with the different modes. It can also be turned on and off via Luup calls. BUT the siren is now added as a generic device that can be armed. 

The siren is not armed in that sense in Vera but tightly connected to the modes. 

To automate siren and the usage of the siren the siren should be treated as a switch.

In __init__.py 
When initiating all devices the siren is not recognised by the right category and is initilized as a VeraDevice. 

My suggestion is to add  the correct category  on row 32

CATEGORY_POWER_METER = 21
CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN = 24
CATEGORY_UV_SENSOR = 28

And add the correct device class for it.  Rows 160-170isch 

                elif device_category == CATEGORY_SCENE_CONTROLLER:
                    device = VeraSceneController(item, self)
                elif device_category == CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN:
                    device = VeraSwitch(item, self)
                else:
                    device = VeraDevice(item, self)
                self.devices.append(device)
                if device_category != CATEGORY_SWITCH and device_category != CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN and device.is_armable:
                    self.devices.append(VeraArmableDevice(item, self))
            else:
                self.devices.append(VeraDevice(item, self))



Im runnig this myself and I can now controll and detect when the siren is invoked with no other changes.

Let me know if this is interesting and if you need more information.

Thanks for great job on this,
Patrik Ekström

issue_title: Add warning about incompatible data types to SequentialFeatureSelector
issue_body:
Currently, the SequentialFeatureSelector yields a `TypeError: unhashable type: 'slice'` error if pandas DataFrames are being used as input rather than NumPy arrays. 

I think that checking/handling pandas DataFrames would require importing pandas, but I think we shouldn't necessarily require users to have pandas installed for running the SequentialFeatureSelector. Thus, it would be better to show a warning if non-NumPy type arrays are used as input

issue_title: Provide option for overwriting existing files
issue_body:
There should exist a procedure which allows for the overwriting of files when attempting to copy files to ZK. Currently, if the file node already exists, an exception is raised, and the execution halted:

> Copy failed; it may have partially completed. To return to a stable state, either fix the issue and re-run the command or manually revert.
> Failure reason:
> znode /path/to/file.yml exists

I can see two possible solutions to this:

1. Rather than raising an exception and exiting, an option for user input could be given:
>         znode /path/to/file.yml exists -- overwrite? [Y/n]: 

2. Offering a dedicated command for overwriting existing files, so that you could choose between `cp` and say, `update` when sending your file.

Excerpt from the [official docs](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/NetworkingInternet/Conceptual/RemoteNotificationsPG/CommunicatingwithAPNs.html) on _Best Practices for Managing Connections_,

> **Keep your connections with APNs open across multiple notifications; do not repeatedly open and close connections. APNs treats rapid connection and disconnection as a denial-of-service attack**. You should leave a connection open unless you know it will be idle for an extended period of time—for example, if you only send notifications to your users once a day, it is acceptable practice to use a new connection each day.

I think it's the best for _apns2-client_ library to conform to this best practice, in order to avoid the possibility of having the services built with the library being seeing as a denial-of-service attack and thus interruption to those services. Therefore, I think we should add a connection pool for managing the reuse of open connections to this library.

For this reason I have decided to use `@pytest.fixture` called `recording_pill`, which at teardown calls `pill.stop()`.

Anyway, I have found, I need to get the session bound to the pill and the only way to do it now is to ask `pill._session` breaking the rule "we are all adults" and accessed the private property.

It would be handy to **have (possibly read-only) `session` property on the `Pill` class.**

Here is my current test suite `tests/test_logs.py`:

    import pytest


    @pytest.fixture
    def session():

        """(py.path.local) directory for recorded responses"""

    def test_recording(recording_pill):

        groups = list(get_groups(client))

    def test_playback(replaying_pill):
        session = replaying_pill._session  # better access public property here
        client = session.client("logs")
        groups = list(get_groups(client))

See this OONI bug: https://trac.torproject.org/projects/tor/ticket/17320

If txtorcon resorts to asking Tor for a GeoIP location for a router and that fails, we should "do something sensible" (like set the location to (None, None)) and handle the Failure.

There is a "python3" tag for any tickets needed to complete Python3 support.

One thing that will _not_ be supported soon is likely the client-side endpoints, as they depend on `txsocksx` at the moment (which is not ported). However, everything else can (and should) be made to work.

See the "python3" branch for a starting-point.

The Unipath API has long been used in production so I'm not inclined to change it much. At the same time, it would be nice to converge on a Python path standard. 
path.py is still around, but the other pre-Unipath distributions have become niche products or fallen away. But now there's PEP 428 (pathlib), which has a different and more modern API. So should we do anything to modernize or converge?

PEP 428 is still in draft status, and as of last year when I talked with Antoine Pitrou, 'pathlib' had not gotten much use so its API couldn't be considered tested. Pathlib was also missing some of Unipath's features. I'm not inclined to break Unipath's backward compatibility because of its long-time use. Yet in my own new applications I'm undecided whether to keep using Unipath, switch to pathlib, or write something new (perhaps porting Unipath's features to pathlib).

So, should we do anything about this?

issue_title: Add USS and (optionally) PSS memory_info_ex process stats
issue_body:
Providing USS would be good because (from http://elinux.org/Android_Memory_Usage):

> Uss is the set of pages that are unique to a process. This is the amount of memory that would be freed if the application was terminated right now.

As such it seems this represents the most important value when profiling the memory usage of a process (a lot more useful than RSS for instance). This is well explained in this article: http://bmaurer.blogspot.it/2006/03/memory-usage-with-smaps.html. There are currently 3 open PRs for adding USS: #744 (Linux), #745 (OSX) and #746 (Windows).

PSS is also useful:

> Pss is the amount of memory shared with other processes, accounted in a way that the amount is divided evenly between the processes that share it. This is memory that would not be released if the process was terminated, but is indicative of the amount that this process is "contributing"

This is something I've been thinking for a while. Problem with current Process implementation is that if somebody wants to fetch multiple (e.g. all) process info the underlying (C / Python) implementation may unnecessarily do the same thing more than once. For instance, in Linux we read `/proc/pid/stat` file to get terminal, cpu_times, and create_time and each time we invoke those methods we `open` the file and `read` from it. Instead this could be done in a single operation. A similar thing happens on other OSes. For instance on BSD we use `kinfo_proc` syscall to get process uid, gid, create_time, memory_info and others. A possible solution would be to provide a context manager which temporarily puts the Process object in a state so that internally the requested metrics are determined in a single shot and then "cached":

```
p = psutil.Process()
with p.oneshot():
       p.terminal()  # internally, this retrieves terminal, cpu_times and create time
       p.cpu_times()  # use the cached value
       p.create_time()  # use the cached value
```

Note: `Process.as_dict()` method would use this method implicitly.
Currently, we loose non-standard elements in the bookmark list, when we do a get-modify-set cycle, as our XSO simply discards unknown elements. This should be fixed.

The following Python snippet solves the task:

    def quat2rot(q):
        "Convert unit quaternion to rotation matrix."
        assert np.isclose(q.norm(), 1.0)
        R = np.array([
        [1 - 2*q.y**2 - 2*q.z**2,   2*q.x*q.y - 2*q.z*q.w,      2*q.x*q.z + 2*q.y*q.w],
        [2*q.x*q.y + 2*q.z*q.w,     1 - 2*q.x**2 - 2*q.z**2,    2*q.y*q.z - 2*q.x*q.w],
        [2*q.x*q.z - 2*q.y*q.w,     2*q.y*q.z + 2*q.x*q.w,      1 - 2*q.x**2 - 2*q.y**2]
        ])
        
        return R

issue_title: Existing bridge interfaces do not store already existing ports
issue_body:
When you add a new bridge interface using pyroute2 and add a port to it, everything works as expected. However, when pyroute2 inherits a bridge interface with ports added to it, those ports don't show up in pyroute2. This can result in a situation where you can add an interface to a bridge twice, and pyroute2 doesn't like it.

Test script:
```python
import pyroute2

netns = pyroute2.NetNS("foo")
ipdb = pyroute2.IPDB(nl=netns)

testdummy0 = None
testbr0 = None

if "testdummy0" in ipdb.by_name.keys():
    testdummy0 = ipdb.interfaces["testdummy0"]
else:
    testdummy0 = ipdb.create(ifname="testdummy0", kind="dummy")
    ipdb.commit()

if "testbr0" in ipdb.by_name.keys():
    testbr0 = ipdb.interfaces["testbr0"]
else:
    testbr0 = ipdb.create(ifname="testbr0", kind="bridge")
    ipdb.commit()

print("testbr0 bridge ports (before): %s" % testbr0.ports)

if testdummy0 not in testbr0.ports:
    print("testdummy0 added")
    testbr0.add_port(testdummy0).commit()
else:
    print("testdummy0 not added")

print("testbr0 bridge ports (after): %s" % testbr0.ports)
```

```
testdummy0 added
```

issue_title: Check for heartbeat acknowledgements, timeout if they are absent.
issue_body: It is possible (and unfortunately not uncommon) for websocket connections to close in such a way that the client never sees the websocket connection close, and "successfully" sends messages into the void, but never receives any responses. The client should check that heartbeats are being acknowledged in a timely fashion, and consider the connection dead if they are not (and attempt a reconnect).

I've been searching for the fastest geodesic library for python and it seems yours is by far the fastest.  This makes me both worried that some edge case is missing and at the same time very impressed :)

If the algorithm is indeed correct I suggest adding unittests and benchmark results to the front page in github.

Here is my benchmark code and results:

```python
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager
from geographiclib.geodesic import Geodesic as geographic_geodesic
import vincenty
import geopy.distance

@contextmanager
def timeit(title, iterations):
    t1 = time.clock()
    yield
    t2 = time.clock()
    elapsed = t2 - t1
    print('%s: elapsed:%0.2fs o/s:%0.2f iterations:%d' % (title, elapsed, iterations / elapsed, iterations))

def time_iter(iterations, func, *args, pre_test=None, **kwargs):
    with timeit("{}.{}".format(func.__module__, func.__name__), iterations):
        for _ in range(iterations):
            if pre_test:
                pre_test()
            val = func(*args, **kwargs)
    print(val)

# latitude/longitude
newport_ri = (41.49008, -71.312796)
cleveland_oh = (41.499498, -81.695391)

def test_geographiclib():
    return geographic_geodesic.WGS84.Inverse(newport_ri[0], newport_ri[1], cleveland_oh[0], cleveland_oh[1], geographic_geodesic.DISTANCE)['s12'] / 1000

def test_geopy():
    return geopy.distance.vincenty(newport_ri, cleveland_oh).kilometers

num_iter = 50000
time_iter(num_iter, test_geopy)
time_iter(num_iter, test_geographiclib)
time_iter(num_iter, vincenty.vincenty, newport_ri, cleveland_oh)
```

results
```
__main__.test_geopy: elapsed:0.24s o/s:41574.34 iterations:10000
866.4554329011002
__main__.test_geographiclib: elapsed:1.71s o/s:5854.28 iterations:10000
866.4554329098687
vincenty.vincenty_inverse: elapsed:0.16s o/s:61436.01 iterations:10000
866.455433
```

as you can see they have similar results, with vincenty reduced by a few decimal digits, however vincenty is 30% faster than geopy and ~10x faster than geographiclib.

issue_title: documentation: passing dynamic role variables to tests when runnning molecule verify
issue_body:
I know this *could* be batted away as something that is related to testinfra only, however, I am finding that I would like to ask here - would you accept a documentation PR that outlines a strategy for including role variables that you passed to your converge playbook, into your unit tests?

Here's what I am currently doing:

```
# molecule/default/playbook.yml
---
- name: Converge
  hosts: all
  vars_files:
    - testvars.yml
  roles:
    - role: my-role
      foobar: "{{ test_foobar }}"
```

Then in a `molecule/default/testvars.yml`, I have:

```
---
test_foobar: barfoo
```

Then, in my `molecule/default/tests/conftest.py`, I do the following:

```
import os

import pytest
from testinfra.utils.ansible_runner import AnsibleRunner

DEFAULT_HOST = 'all'

inventory = os.environ['MOLECULE_INVENTORY_FILE']
runner = AnsibleRunner(inventory)
runner.get_hosts(DEFAULT_HOST)


@pytest.fixture
def testvars(host):
    variables = runner.run(
        DEFAULT_HOST,
        'include_vars',
        'testvars.yml'
    )
    return variables['ansible_facts']
```

And in my tests, I can then:

```
def test_something(host, testvars):
    print(testvars['test_foobar'])
```

As far as I can see, this is the cleanest way (I've digged around in a lot of tickets) of matching up the dynamic variables that you pass to your role and your testinfra pytest tests.

If it isn't, please someone tell me :)

In any case, the main question remains - is there a place we can start to document this on the RTD setup?

Would it be feasible to have wildcard support for exclude_paths? Or even have another parameter passed with regex expression that would be used with re.match to figure out if the current path is excluded

```
{

}
```

issue_title: Pager ajaxProcessing outputting rows in object order, not based on key name.
issue_body:
I'm using TableSorter 2.19.1 as of 2/11/2015.  I'm attempting to use the pager function w/ajax like the example posted at:

https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-pager-ajax.html

I don't have any control over the "row" object sort order unless I manually generate the JSON as string.  It's as if the name/pair values weren't being used..  If each value is named, it shouldn't have to be in the same order.  What I'm finding is that the table is being updated with the object key order in each row instead of using the keys.

In other words, these 2 objects will output differently from one another even though the name/value pairs contain the same data but are only outputted in a different order:

    {
        "ID": 1,
        "Name": "Kabul",

    }

    {
        "Name": "Kabul",
        "CountryCode": "AFG",
        "ID": 1,
        "Population": 1780000
        "District": "Kabol",
    }

The sample code should be updated to loop over the column names, determine if a named value exists in the row object and then add it to the array in that order instead of just looping through available object items. In the case that an item doesn't exist, add an empty cell.

issue_body:
With the master branch:

I'm using the RS256 algorithm, but I expect this will fail when using any of the other asymmetrical algorithms.  When accessing `/auth/verify` with the token, I get the failure:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 556, in handle_request
    response = await response
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/blueprint.py", line 102, in verify
    request, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/authentication.py", line 169, in verify
    payload = self._decode(token, verify=verify)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/authentication.py", line 62, in _decode
    **kwargs
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jwt.py", line 78, in decode
    jwt, key=key, algorithms=algorithms, options=options, **kwargs
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jws.py", line 140, in decode
    key, algorithms)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jws.py", line 206, in _verify_signature
    if not alg_obj.verify(signing_input, key, signature):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/algorithms.py", line 317, in verify
    key.verify(sig, msg, padding.PKCS1v15(), self.hash_alg())
AttributeError: '_RSAPrivateKey' object has no attribute 'verify'
```

From my reading, this is because you need to use the public key to verify the token, since it was signed with the private key.  But sanic-jwt doesn't have a way to set the public-key.  It just has SANIC_JWT_SECRET.

https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/issues/81

In https://github.com/bastikr/boolean.py/blob/master/boolean/boolean.py#L860:

```python
if all(arg in self.args for arg in item.args):
    args = tuple(arg for arg in self.args if arg not in expo)
```
It uses an undefined variable `item`. What is this supposed to do?

I just removed the `all()` test, leaving only this:

```python
args = tuple(arg for arg in self.args if arg not in expo)
```

But I'm not sure what that check was intended for (originally).

issue_body:
Hi, I have a strange one, whereby I seem to be losing exceptions from MS SQL Server.
When I invoke a stored procedure, using the 'ProofOfPyodbcError.py' (attached), I receive exceptions up until I do some sort of Update DDL in the Stored Procedure.
Prior to the Insert/Update/Delete, I can receive exceptions.

The ProofOfPyodbcError.py script has hard coded Database connection string, so it will need to be tweaked if you wish to run it.

After the Insert/Update/Delete, it doesn't matter what I do, no exceptions are received by the python code.

The attached Proof_Of_Error_DDL.sql will establish a test table and stored procedure, although, the script does have a hard coded Database name in it.

The ODBC settings are done using C:\Windows\SysWOW64\odbcad32.exe to create a "System DSN", which is used in the Database connection string.

issue_title: Ragged Arrays of Complex User Types
issue_body:
It turns out there is another, _really_ nasty use case for the automated unit testing that isn't handled yet. Suppose I have a derived type such as:

```fortran
type nasty
  integer :: A(:,:)
  logical :: B(:)
  complex :: C(:,:,:)
end type nasty
```

Next, suppose that the subroutine I am unit testing uses a 3D array of these guys:

```fortran
subroutine uses_nasty(narray, oarray)
  type(nasty), intent(in) :: narray(:,:,:)
  type(nasty), intent(out) :: oarray(:,:)
end subroutine uses_nasty
```

To initialize the input parameter, we would need a bunch of files storing the arrays. For example `A.in.{}`, `B.in.{}` and `C.in.{}`. Since `C` is already 3D, there is no hope of doing this using a ragged array setup. Instead, we could use `<part>` tags to nest the assignment of the values to these members of the derived type `nasty`.

```xml
<assignment name="narray" value="p1" allocate="10,5,2">
  <value identifier="A" file="A.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="A" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="A.in" />
  <value identifier="B" file="B.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="B" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="B.in" />
  <value identifier="C" file="C.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="C" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="C.in" />
  <part identifier="p1">
    <part identifier="p2">
      <part identifier="p3" value="A, B, C" />
    </part>
  </part>
</assignment>
```

The new _suffix_ attribute specifies the text (where loop variable values are substituted for the `$i` values) to replace `*` with in the file name when searching for the source to load a variable's value from. When the driver is generated, a call to `fpy_period_join_indices` will generate the suffix and it will be spliced in by python in the right place using `filename.replace("*", "// varname_fpy_suffix //")`. The wildcard in the filename will still ensure that the existing routines will copy all the necessary files to the execution directory for a given test case. The above XML says to allocate `narray(10,5,2)` and then create a loop system:

```fortran
do narray_fpy_1=1, size(narray, 1)
  do narray_fpy_2=1, size(narray, 2)
    do narray_fpy_3=1, size(narray, 3)
      call fpy_period_join_indices(narray_fpy_suffix, (/ narray_fpy_1, narray_fpy_2, narray_fpy_3 /), 3)
      call fpy_read(narray%A, "A.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
      call fpy_read(narray%B, "B.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
      call fpy_read(narray%C, "C.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
    end do
  end do
end do
```

Things to do:

1. Add support for the _suffix_ attribute to the `<value>` tags. Also the _member_ attribute is still awaiting implementation.
2. Replace the `*` with the appropriate suffix code (which includes initializing a new suffix variable and calling `fpy_period_join_indices`) in all calls to `fpy_read`.
3. Allow a comma-separated list of `<value>` identifiers to be specified for `<part>` _and_ `<assignment>` tags. The order they appear in the comma-separated list determines the order in which they are executed. Thus, a user could assign values to `A, B, C` above using those `<value>` tags and then still call an embedded function (for example).

issue_title: Test failing due to flake8 update
issue_body:
Just for info tests are failing due to a pytest-flake8 update of some sort

I'll do a fix

Let's say somebody made a `task` from scratch.
Let's also say `task` contains date placeholders and could look something like this.
```
date['entry'] = '20171005T003442Z'
date['modified'] = '20171005T003442Z'
```

Now what? To make date placeholders usable, we import datetime and come up with `strptime` or `strftime` formatting ourselves. We create `datetime` format configs for them too.

```
format_entry: specify time/date strftime to use (default None)
format_modified: specify time/date strftime to use (default None)
```

```
format_entry = '%a %b %d'     # Fri Oct 06
format_modified = '%a %b %d'  # Fri Oct 06

```

I was thinking maybe we need a date threshold?
```
self.py3.threshold_get_date(task['entry'], 'entry', DATETIME_FORMAT)
self.py3.threshold_get_date(task['modified'], 'modified', DATETIME_FORMAT)

dates = ['%a %b']
dates = {'entry': '%a %b', 'modified': '%b %d'}

format_task = '{description} ({modified})'
```
```
Buy milk (Oct 06)
```
Ideally, this should support relative time too. Supposedly it can be useful for `task`'s `{modified}` placeholder... and probably for other modules too, eg...
```
Buy milk (updated 40 minutes ago)
Buy milk (40 min ago)
Buy milk (40m ago)
Buy milk (1d ago)
(etc etc etc)
```

Now I wonder if we need something like `deprecation of configuration parameters`
for config section where we could specifiy...
* color thresholds
* date thresholds
* turning floats to integers. skips {example:3.0f} in the format)
* replacing / converting
* others

I don't know. Something to brainstorm.

issue_title: Make sorting by unsigned integers the natsorted default?
issue_body:
I am making the proposal to change the default sorting algorithm for `natsorted` starting with version 4.0.0.  Currently, `natsorted` looks for any string of numbers that are valid input to the `float` function in a greedy way (for example, `"number-1.402e10hello"` would be split into `['number', -14020000000.0, 'hello']`).  

I have observed that this does not pass the "rule of least astonishment" for most users because it is easy to not recognize that `'-'` and `'.'` are actually valid parts of a number or that the algorithm would be accounting for signs and decimals, and thus users are surprised to see `natsorted` return results where `"version2.10" < "version2.2"` or `"version-2.0" < "version-1.0"`.  I have observed this in [this Stack Overflow Question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24045348/sort-set-of-numbers-in-the-form-xx-yy-in-python), [this issue reported to natsort](https://github.com/SethMMorton/natsort/issues/19), and [this python module that was created because `natsorted` did not meet their expectations and instead of filing a bug report attacked natsort in its documentation](https://github.com/xolox/python-naturalsort) (the documentation was toned down in the 3rd commit).

My proposal would be to make the behavior of the current function `versorted` be the default behavior for `natsorted`, and make the current `natsorted` behavior belong to a new function (possibly called `realnatsorted` but I am open to suggestions).  `versorted` would remain for backwards compatibility.  A `SIGNED` attribute to the `ns` algorithm chooser class would have to be added.  This would mean that `natsorted` would by default return results where `"version2.2" < "version2.10"` and `"version-1.0" < "version-2.0"` which I believe would meet most user's expectations.

I am polling the community to find out if I am correct in my assumption that people would expect `natsorted` to only look for digits and not `'-'` and `'.'` from a string, and if it would be worth it to modify the `natsorted` default behavior to provide less astonishment without breaking existing code too severely.

issue_title: Git commit fails on Windows 7/8.1 with TypeError: environment can only contain strings
issue_body:
I'm trying to use bumpversion on Windows, but I get an error when I try to autocommit with git. I believe the problem is in BaseVCS.commit with the following code:

```
subprocess.check_output(cls._COMMIT_COMMAND + [f.name], env=dict(
    list(os.environ.items()) + [('HGENCODING', 'utf-8')]
))
```

Dumping env from within subprocess.py gives the following output:
```
{

...

 'FRAMEWORKVERSION': 'v4.0.30319',
 'FRAMEWORKVERSION32': 'v4.0.30319',
 'FSHARPINSTALLDIR': 'C:\\Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft SDKs\\F#\\3.1\\Framework\\v4.0\\',
 u'HGENCODING': u'utf-8',
 'HOME': 'C:\\Users\\rgray',
 'HOMEPATH': '\\',

...

}
```

Note that HGENCODING seems to have been added to the dict as unicode. If I force it to ascii as follows then it works as expected:

```
subprocess.check_output(cls._COMMIT_COMMAND + [f.name], env=dict(
    list(os.environ.items()) + [('HGENCODING'.encode('ascii'), 'utf-8'.encode('ascii'))]
))
```

Hi again @sepandhaghighi, one more minor issue:

the examples in the README are great for demonstrating functionality, but I think examples demonstrating actual use cases would be helpful for people.

Again, there are many metrics included in the package; I would say pick three-five metrics that you think will be the ones most frequently used by all users, and then provide some more real-world examples of using pycm with those metrics.
E.g., using it in conjunction with scikit-learn to compare two or three different classifiers.

This would be a place that a simple visualization would really be helpful and make the concepts more intuitive.
I have often adapted this code from the scikit-learn docs to my own needs when I have to visualize a confusion matrix:

If you do not want to add scikit-learn as a dependency, you might just add a `data` sub-package with outputs from some models you have trained, and the same goes for matplotlib or whatever you use to visualize, you could for now just have the plots as static .png files in your library.

Maybe there was a particular problem you were facing that inspired you to develop the package and you have true data + predictions from those models that you could include as example datasets?

I really think adding these sorts of examples will help you recruit users to whom it's not immediately obvious why they shouldn't just use the basic confusion_matrix functionality in scikit-learn.

issue_title: Ping value on UNIX systems may be inacurate
issue_body:
In the following function :


```
def get_best_server(self, servers=None):
        if not servers:
            if not self.closest:
                servers = self.get_closest_servers()
            servers = self.closest

        results = {}
        for server in servers:
            cum = []
            url = os.path.dirname(server['url'])
            urlparts = urlparse('%s/latency.txt' % url)
            printer('%s %s/latency.txt' % ('GET', url), debug=True)
            for _ in range(0, 3):
                try:
                    if urlparts[0] == 'https':
                        h = HTTPSConnection(urlparts[1])
                    else:
                        h = HTTPConnection(urlparts[1])
                    headers = {'User-Agent': USER_AGENT}
                    start = timeit.default_timer()
                    h.request("GET", urlparts[2], headers=headers)
                    r = h.getresponse()
                    total = (timeit.default_timer() - start)
                except HTTP_ERRORS:
                    e = get_exception()
                    printer('%r' % e, debug=True)
                    cum.append(3600)
                    continue

                text = r.read(9)
                if int(r.status) == 200 and text == 'test=test'.encode():
                    cum.append(total)
                else:
                    cum.append(3600)
                h.close()

            avg = round((sum(cum) / 6) * 1000.0, 3)
            results[avg] = server

        try:
            fastest = sorted(results.keys())[0]
        except IndexError:
            raise SpeedtestBestServerFailure('Unable to connect to servers to '
                                             'test latency.')
        best = results[fastest]
        best['latency'] = fastest

        self.results.ping = fastest
        self.results.server = best

        self.best.update(best)
        printer(best, debug=True)
        return best


```

Ping is measured using timeit which can see interference from other processes running on the same machine. This is according to the python documentation found here: https://docs.python.org/2/library/timeit.html

> timeit.default_timer()¶
Define a default timer, in a platform-specific manner. On Windows, time.clock() has microsecond granularity, but time.time()’s granularity is 1/60th of a second. On Unix, time.clock() has 1/100th of a second granularity, and time.time() is much more precise. On either platform, default_timer() measures wall clock time, not the CPU time. This means that other processes running on the same computer may interfere with the timing.


Also, ping appears to be measured using an HTTP GET request,  not the regular ICMP ping. Can you verify if this is the case?

The fix for #162 uses `env -0`, which is a GNU extension that isn't part of POSIX, BSD, etc., which means a whole lot of things now break on OS X, FreeBSD, etc.

As far as I know, there is no perfect portable fix for #162; for example, the standard `env` command will produce the exact same output for these two cases:

    env a=foo b=bar
    env a='foo
    b=bar'

As far as I know, the only way to do it reliably and portably (at least to anything that's compatible with BSD3 or later, which is a lot more than just GNU…) from the shell is to first get a (potential) list of names by calling and parsing `env` or `printenv`, and then lookup each name one by one with `printenv`.

Of course that can obviously cause performance problems. You can solve that for GNU systems by trying `env -0` first and only falling back if that fails:

        env0 = session.run("env -0; echo")
        if env0[0] == 0 and not env 0[2].rstrip():
            self._curr = dict(line.split('=', 1)
                              for line in env0[1].split('\x00')
                              if '=' in line)
        else:
            lines = self.remote._session.run("env; echo")[1].splitlines()
            split = (line.split('=', 1) for line in lines)
            keys = (line[0] for line in split if len(line)>1)
            runs = ((key, session.run('printenv "%s"; echo' % key))
                    for key in keys)
            self._curr = dict((key, run[1].rstrip('\n')) for (key, run) in runs
                              if run[0] == 0 and run[1].rstrip('\n')
                              and not run[2])

Alternatively, for platforms without `env -0`, you can use some heuristics on the output of `env`, which aren't perfect, but may be good enough for most users.

Your existing code already skips any line without a `=`; this will truncate variables with embedded newlines, and it will mean that `a='foo\nb=bar'` will give you a fake variable `b`, but maybe that's acceptable.

You can eliminate the first problem by treating any line that doesn't start with a valid name followed by `=` as a continuation of the previous line, as in pigay's suggested patch for #162.

But there's no way to eliminate the last problem. I don't *think* that's likely to fail too often by accident… but you might want to think about whether someone could engineer some exploit that takes advantage of it.

issue_title: Give floats() and decimals() allow_nan and allow_infinity flags.
issue_body:
In order to preserve the current behaviour these should default to True. When set to False, the strategies should not generate NaN or infinite values respectively.

A decent first pass at this would be to just use these flags to filter after the existing generation. It would be good to add these flags to the NastyFloats constructor explicitly though.

decimals() can just pass this through to floats() I think.
The Installation instructions should contain `pip --user` and advanced instructions for custom install (i.e. with a module system)

bitarray's __setitem__ checks the existing bounds:

>>> b = bitarray.bitarray()
>>> i = 3
>>> b[i] = True
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
IndexError: bitarray index out of range

For sparser bitarrays, it would be nice to be able to set an index (or a sequence of indices) to 1, resizing if necessary to accommodate the extra bits.

This can be done with:
>>> b.extend(itertools.repeat(0, i+1-b.length()))
>>> b[i] = True
>>> b
bitarray('0001')

But it would be nice if there was a clean an efficient way to do that sort of thing.

issue_title: Boolean should validate rather than coerce values
issue_body:
This issue is about a design decision regarding `Boolean`.

The following schema:

```python
s = Any(Boolean(), None)
```

intends to express the idea of "any boolean value or `None`". This seems reasonable but for the value `None` it evaluates to `False`:


```python
>>> s(None)
False
```

This happens because `Boolean` coerces the value using `bool()` and therefore "validates" the value. Although it makes sense that `Any` checks the validators from left to right, this result can be an unexpected result if one has not read the docstring of `Boolean`. For the values `True` and `False` this schema also succeeds but it returns the values `True` and `False` as expected.

It seems that `Boolean` differs from other validation objects in the voluptuous library by doing  validation for string values but coercion for non-string values.

It would be more explicit to let users express coercion using `Coerce(bool)` and to implement `Boolean` as a validator:

```python
@message('expected boolean', cls=BooleanInvalid)
def Boolean2(v):
    if isinstance(v, basestring):
        v = v.lower()
        if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
            return True
        if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
            return False
        raise ValueError
    if not isinstance(v, bool):
        raise ValueError
    return v
```

The schema mentioned earlier can be fixed by doing:

```python
s = Any(None, Boolean())
```

but it does mean that a new developer could add another component after `Boolean()` and introduce a bug.

I tried a quick fix, changing these lines to something like
```python

self.log.debug("Parsing group vars (dir): {0}".format(path))
```

issue_title: Smart Iteration should deal with PK holes
issue_body:
Iterating over a large table with PK holes - i.e. where there are big gaps - can lead to very bad iteration where the chunk size gets stuck small.

More of the intelligence from pt-online-schema-change could be copied, e.g. where they (I think) `EXPLAIN` every chunk before fetching it: http://bazaar.launchpad.net/~percona-toolkit-dev/percona-toolkit/2.2/view/head:/bin/pt-online-schema-change#L5245

As reported by @lwiecek

# Summary

There are already some numeric functions provided, like `Abs()`, `Ceiling()` and `Round()`. A lot of MySQL's mathematical functions are missing though, like `POW()` or `RADIANS()` (see [list of MySQL's mathematical functions](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/mathematical-functions.html)).

Would be nice to have them inside functions.py so you could easily import them instead of defining them for yourself. Although there is already a [Django PR](https://github.com/django/django/pull/9622) for this, it doesn't seem to be pushed soon.

# Configuration

Django Version: Django 2.0.0

Database and version used: MySQL 5.7

Version: Django-MySQL 2.2.2
Similar to #10, MySQL's `FIND_IN_SET` could be used to implement model fields that serialize to a list, as a poor man's parallel of the postgres array fields that `django.contrib.postgres` has.

Seen happening on an iterator in a test case which iterated very quickly:

issue_title: Node select & callable replace interface like re.sub
issue_body:
It would be useful to have an interface similar to `re.sub` that allows matching and in-place modifications of wikitext.

pywikibot has many instances of regex's being used, and the regex's are nasty to become close to accurate, and still are not perfect.  Ideally we could combined / replace them with `mwparserfromhell`, and it would be useful if the interface was similar.

`Wikicode.replace` is a good start, providing reasonable selection.   However `value` currently must be a specific value of some kind.  If value could be callable, and the matched node provided as an argument to the call, `Wikicode.replace` could be used to dynamically determine the replacement value based on the context matched.

issue_title: Setting user.is_active to false at end of pipeline logs out user
issue_body:
Hi 

I'm trying to implement custom email validation at the end of the pipeline with Django 1.7.4, using the same logic as for regular non-social user registrations:

* Set user.is_active to false
* Generate and send an email with an activation link
* Redirect to success page where the user is logged in but not active, so the context can display the user email that has been captured.

At the end of the pipeline I have a validate partial function that does this:

    @partial
    def validate(strategy, details, user=None, is_new=False, *args, **kwargs):
	if user and is_new:

		user.is_active = False
		user.save()

		username = user.username
		email = user.email
		salt = hashlib.sha1(str(random.random()).encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()[:5]            
		activation_key = hashlib.sha1(str(salt + email).encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()            
		key_expires = datetime.datetime.today() + datetime.timedelta(2)

		new_profile = UserProfile(user=user, activation_key=activation_key, key_expires=key_expires)
		new_profile.save()

		email_subject = 'Account confirmation'
		email_body = "Hey %s, thanks for signing up. To activate your account, click this link within 48 hours %s%s" % (username, site(strategy.request)['site_root'], reverse('users:confirm', args=[activation_key]))
		send_mail(email_subject, email_body, settings.DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL, [email], fail_silently=False)
		return
	
	return

And in settings I have this:

    SOCIAL_AUTH_INACTIVE_USER_LOGIN = True
    INACTIVE_USER_URL = '/signup/success/'

The `INACTIVE_USER_URL` is being picked up, but the user is not logged in.

Any idea why this wouldn't work as expected?

Thanks!

Russell.

issue_title: Methods using async_contextmanager decorator don't pass pylint
issue_body:
I have a context manager method using the `async_contextmanager` decorator:

```
@asyncio_extras.async_contextmanager
async def scoped_session():
    session = Session()
    try:
        yield session
        session.commit()
    except:
        session.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        session.close()
```

And then I use this context manager like so:

```
async def create_api_key(module):
    async with scoped_session() as session:
        pass
    pass
```

When I try to run this through `pylint`, it fails, being unable to find the context manager object's `__aenter__` and `__aexit__` methods.

```
$ pylint -E file.py
No config file found, using default configuration
************* Module file
E:  6,10: Async context manager 'generator' doesn't implement __aenter__ and __aexit__. (not-async-context-manager)
```

issue_title: remove_all_matching(None, name) broken in 0.3.4 if hosts file contains comments.
issue_body:
PR #6 that was merged in 03.4 breaks remove_all_matching for line identified as comments.

Since entry.names is initialized as None by default, comment entries have a None value in the names property. The filtering used in remove_all_matching() in 0.3.4 breaks in those cases.

To replicate simply have a hosts file with comments and invoke remove_all_matching(None, name) to remove an entry by name.

Just had to work this out for myself. If you're dependent on logstash for keeping records, then unhandled exceptions should really be shipped there too. I did this by calling the method below in my setup:

    def config_global_exception_handling():
        '''
        Override the global exception hook to come here.
        Here, log the exception which includes sending to logstash.
        '''
        def customexcepthook(exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback):
            sys.exc_info = lambda: (exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback)
            logging.exception('Unhandled exception')
        sys.excepthook = customexcepthook

"Unhandled exception" becomes the _message_ attribute, and the _stack_trace_ attribute gets populated appropriately

In pytest_sanic.utils.TestServer.close(), there is this code:

```
            # Force close connections
            coros = []
            for conn in self.connections:
                if hasattr(conn, "websocket") and conn.websocket:
>                   coros.append(conn.websocket.close_connection(after_handshake=False))
E                   TypeError: close_connection() got an unexpected keyword argument 'after_handshake'
```
As you can see from the `E` error here, conn.websocket.close_connection() doesn't expect an after_handshake arg.


I started with self certificate and it works. With letsencrypt's certificate no.
This is my bot code:

```
#!/usr/bin/python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

# This is a simple echo bot using decorators and webhook with BaseHTTPServer
# It echoes any incoming text messages and does not use the polling method.

import BaseHTTPServer
import ssl
import telebot
import logging


API_TOKEN = 'MYTOKEN'

WEBHOOK_HOST = 'mydomain.net'
WEBHOOK_PORT = 8443  # 443, 80, 88 or 8443 (port need to be 'open')
WEBHOOK_LISTEN = '0.0.0.0'  # In some VPS you may need to put here the IP addr

WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT = '/etc/letsencrypt/live/mydomain.net/fullchain.pem'  # Path to the ssl certificate
WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV = '/etc/letsencrypt/live/mydomain.net/privkey.pem'  # Path to the ssl private key

##SELF works
#WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT = '/etc/opt/webhook_cert.pem'  # Path to the ssl certificate
#WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV = '/etc/opt/webhook_pkey.pem'  # Path to the ssl private key

WEBHOOK_URL_BASE = "https://%s:%s" % (WEBHOOK_HOST, WEBHOOK_PORT)
WEBHOOK_URL_PATH = "/%s/" % (API_TOKEN)


logger = telebot.logger
telebot.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

bot = telebot.TeleBot(API_TOKEN)


# WebhookHandler, process webhook calls
class WebhookHandler(BaseHTTPServer.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    server_version = "WebhookHandler/1.0"

    def do_HEAD(self):
        self.send_response(200)
        self.end_headers()

    def do_GET(self):
        self.send_response(200)
        self.end_headers()

    def do_POST(self):
        if self.path == WEBHOOK_URL_PATH and \
           'content-type' in self.headers and \
           'content-length' in self.headers and \
           self.headers['content-type'] == 'application/json':
            json_string = self.rfile.read(int(self.headers['content-length']))

            self.send_response(200)
            self.end_headers()

            update = telebot.types.Update.de_json(json_string)
            bot.process_new_messages([update.message])
        else:
            self.send_error(403)
            self.end_headers()


# Handle '/start' and '/help'
@bot.message_handler(commands=['help', 'start'])
def send_welcome(message):
    bot.reply_to(message,
                 ("Hi there, I am EchoBot.\n"
                  "I am here to echo your kind words back to you."))


# Handle all other messages
@bot.message_handler(func=lambda message: True, content_types=['text'])
def echo_message(message):
    bot.reply_to(message, message.chat.id)

# Remove webhook, it fails sometimes the set if there is a previous webhook
bot.remove_webhook()

# Set webhook
bot.set_webhook(url=WEBHOOK_URL_BASE+WEBHOOK_URL_PATH,
                certificate=open(WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT, 'r'))

# Start server
httpd = BaseHTTPServer.HTTPServer((WEBHOOK_LISTEN, WEBHOOK_PORT),
                                  WebhookHandler)

httpd.socket = ssl.wrap_socket(httpd.socket,
                               certfile=WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT,
                               keyfile=WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV,
                               server_side=True)

httpd.serve_forever()

```

issue_title: Using get_user() in the UserDatastore instead of find_user()
issue_body:
## Issue:
I am extending the login/register routine and customizing the UserDatastore using username in addition to email (although this question would apply more to those not using an email) with custom Groups and other Items.  When automating role creation via signals (user_registered or user_confirmed) and passing the user to [add_role_to_user(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L99) it passes to [_prepare_role_modify_args(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L70) which in turn uses the [find_user](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L72) method which looks up only for the email for that user and returns none if something else is passed to it.  In my case I was passing the username, which causes it to find none. 

As mentioned I am extending the current UserDatastore and using similar methods for adding Groups and Projects/Items which have similar relationships to the User Model and thus using similar methods to do so.  I am using the [get_user()](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L87) method to capture my User Model which looks for the [indentity](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/utils.py#L377) as set in [configuration variable](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/core.py#L92) which returns the user model the same as the find_user() method.

## Question: 
Is there an advantage to using find_user() over get_user() in the role lookup?  It would seem using the get_user() method you would make it a bit easier to customize and would solve some of the issues with custom implementations that I have seen here and provide for more flexibility for the developer and extensibility in the future.

If get_user() was used instead of find_user() would it break something somewhere else or cause some compatibility issues that I am not seeing?  It would appear that get_user() is extended to the Peewee and MongoDB Classes and appear to return exactly the same as the SQL Class. 

This caused me a little bit of a headache figuring out, however I am able to use it as is with my current setup.  If you were not using an email in your model it would not be possible to use this method to add a user to a role and would require you to bypass large portions of the script to make your customization work.


I am happy make the changes and submit a PR if there are no objections.

https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/a0e203774795f1bad289d76357237fcb604bfa01/flask_security/utils.py#L82

Isn't the whole point of Flask-Login interface for `get_id()` to avoid calling an id attribute directly? I'm implementing a backend for Google datastore which doe snot have id attibute.

issue_title: Is it possible to have multiple instance of the jwt token
issue_body: Is it possible to protect a route with different instance of JWT token?

According to this article (https://dev.to/neilmadden/7-best-practices-for-json-web-tokens) and others, including the header on a JWT might be a security vulnerability at worst and redundant at best:
>A JWT consists of a protected payload together with a plaintext "header" section. This can contain various bits of information such as the algorithms used to sign or encrypt the payload or application-specific information to be used by intermediaries on the network, e.g. for message routing. In a lot of cases, this information is redundant and it is downright dangerous to trust its contents anyway. If you do not need to interoperate with third parties that expect standard JWTs, you can save some space and eliminate a whole class of vulnerabilities by simply stripping off the header section when producing a JWT and then recreate it from known data before parsing. I call these "headless JWTs" and recommend you use them wherever you can.
Stripping the header is easy: just remove everything up to the first "." character in the encoded JWT. To reconstruct the JWT, just base64url-encode a fixed header identifying the known algorithm and parameters and prepend it to the headless JWT.

I have been working with an implementation of this feature which uses the app context to store the header, but I'm not sure this would be the best implementation. If this a feature people would like, I can continue development on this feature.

```
var socket = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:8000/?token=фффàè')
```

issue_title: error when add RandomCropAug into aug_list during initialization of ImageIter
issue_body:
Hi, I think there's a bug when you want to apply random cropping in ImageIter in python as follows:

> mx.image.ImageIter(batch_size=32, data_shape=(3, 244, 244), aug_list=[mx.image.RandomCropAug(128, 128)], ...)

since the shape of each batch is set in advance(image/image.py, line 1186-1187):
> batch_data = nd.empty((batch_size, c, h, w))
> batch_label = nd.empty(self.provide_label[0][1])

in image/image.py, line 1201, the processed image is directly assigned back to batch_data:
> batch_data[i] = self.postprocess_data(data)

Since the shapes of batch_data and the cropped image are different if you apply random cropping, the cropped image cannot be broadcasted and will raise an error:
> ValueError: operands could not be broadcast together with remapped shapes[original->remapped]: (3, 128, 128) and requested shape (3, 244, 244)

I think we can check if RandomCropAug or RandomSizedCropAug are in aug_list first, if so, the shape of batch_data should be set to the size of cropped image and I'm working on it.

issue_title: Deadlock in LibMxNet.dll during unload due to synchronization call in destructor
issue_body:
Note: Providing complete information in the most concise form is the best way to get help. This issue template serves as the checklist for essential information to most of the technical issues and bug reports. For non-technical issues and feature requests, feel free to present the information in what you believe is the best form.

For Q & A and discussion, please start a discussion thread at https://discuss.mxnet.io 

## Description
The destructor for "mxnet::Engine" local static instance  calls into STL's condition_variable::notify_all , which in turn calls  NtReleaseKeyedEvent, an undocumented Windows API which results in deadlock, as the call is being run with loader lock acquired, in the context of DLL unload machinery.
Independent of this hang, having non-trivial destructor for static local variable is recipe for unforeseen problems. These destructors are called in unspecified order, and may call into other DLLs or ( Shared Objects) that are unloaded.   

(Brief description of the problem in no more than 2 sentences.)

## Environment info (Required)
Windows 7, any MxNet build  

Compiler (gcc/clang/mingw/visual studio):

## Error Message:
No error message, the Python process hangs upon exit

## Minimum reproducible example
(If you are using your own code, please provide a short script that reproduces the error. Otherwise, please provide link to the existing example.)

## Steps to reproduce
1. Set up a standard Windows 7 system 
2. Install Python from https://sourceforge.net/projects/winpython/files/WinPython_3.6/3.6.5.1/beta... ... 
3. Run the following in the Windows Python command shell: 
a. pip install mxnet 
b. python 
4. Run the following in the Python shell: 
a. import mxnet 
b. exit() 

## What have you tried to solve it?
Proposed solution 

a. Provide explicit API to destroy the "mxnet::Engine" instance that is complement to Engine::Create. 
b. Have LibMxNet consumers call Destroy API explicitly before exiting. 
c. Modify the destructor to not perform cleanup, as Destroy will have already done that
d. If no one calls cleanup, it should not matter. As, after the process is destroyed, all the resources will be reclaimed by the OS

issue_title: MXNet from Source is slower than Pip Package
issue_body:
Hi there,

I have two copies of `MXNet` - one local copy that I kept for revision and another global copy which has been installed using `sudo -H pip3 install mxnet-cu80==0.12.1`. I built my local copy using the following command:

```
make -j $(nproc) \
	USE_OPENCV=1 USE_BLAS=openblas USE_CUDA=1 \
	USE_CUDA_PATH=/usr/local/cuda USE_CUDNN=1 USE_CPP_PACKAGE=1
```

and created a virtual environment for the python binding. The problem is that - when I run the `cudnn_lstm_bucketing.py` example, what I noticed is that the local copy (throughput is around `3600 samples/sec`) is around `1000 samples/sec` slower than the global copy (throughput is around `4600 samples/sec`).

Could someone please tell me whether I am missing some build options in my `make` command? Thank you.

ref: https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/initializing-parameters-of-symbolblock/1213

It looks like SymbolBlock will initializing all the parameters using the initializer given in the block.initialize() function, including bias, which cannot be initialized by Xavier. 

Alought that [post](https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/initializing-parameters-of-symbolblock/1213) suggests a solution, and in addition, I think we should try to initialize the parameters if it was given in the symbol/json file.

e.g. 
`bias = mx.sym.Variable(name='conv_bias', shape=(num_filter,), init=mx.init.Zero())`
or
```
{
      "op": "null", 
      "name": "conv_bias", 
      "attrs": {
        "__init__": "[\"zero\", {}]", 
        "__shape__": "(64,)"
      }, 
      "inputs": []
}
```

Or if there a better way please let me know.

issue_title: Race condition when MKLDNN is enabled
issue_body:
When MKLDNN is enabled, the output of any MKLDNN op will have MKL layout. If such ndarray is passed to any operator registered with `FComputeEx` interface, calling `NDArray::data` will trigger "Reorder2Default" which may cause race condition since it doesn't prevent others from writing to it at the same time. 

The number of non-MKL ops registered with `FComputeEx` is over 50. This should be fixed and explicitly tested.

issue_title: bravado-core does not support multiple parameters properly
issue_body:
We're experiencing an issue where `cast_request_param` is failing with a `TypeError` in the following scenario. For the record, we use pyramid_swagger and by virtue of that, bravado-core.

If you have a spec like:
```
"/objects":
    get:
      operationId: list_objects
      tags:
        - Events
      parameters:
        - in: query
          name: parent_id
          required: true
          description: The ID of the Parent you would like to list all Objects for
          type: integer
          format: int64
```

Then make a request with:
```
GET /resource?parent_id=123&parent_id=456
```

bravado-core will raise a `TypeError` and therefore a 500 because we're trying to cast `['123', '456']` to via `int()`.

I may be misunderstanding how [parameter descriptions](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/2-0/describing-parameters/) work in Swagger 2.0, but from my perspective, this seems like a problem in the library.

A simple fix to not return 500s, would be to also catch `TypeError` and then allow jsonschema to handle the validation (as suggested by the comment [here](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/54d099e3fa1df0fe027908e96dd3718b11b689bf/bravado_core/param.py#L257)), which would then would return a 400 instead. This seems more proper. I'll put up a PR to address the issue, but happy to discuss further in case I'm mistaken.

Looking forward to hearing from you @sjaensch!

I've tested several what is the cause of problem. There are 3 factors I could think of. Architecture, Django web server, and wkhtmltopdf version.

1. Ubuntu 12.04 32 bit, FastCGI, Build from source 0.12.1 => Work!
2. Ubuntu 12.04 32 bit, uWSGI, Build from source 0.12.1 => Work!
3. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, FastCGI, Build from source 0.12.1 => Work!
4. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, uWSGI, Build from source 0.12.1 => Doesn't Work!
5. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, python manage.py runserver, Build from source 0.12.1 => Work!
6. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, FastCGI, Download from website 0.12.2 => Work!
7. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, uWSGI, Download from website 0.12.2 => Doesn't Work!
8. Ubuntu 14.10 64 bit, python manage.py runserver, Build from source 0.12.1 => Work!

Summary from above:
1. Use FastCGI (It is going to deprecate when Django 1.9 comes) or python manage.py runserver always WORK!
2. If architecture is 64 bit, it wont' WORK with uWSGI.

I would love to see uWSGI could work on 64 bit server.

issue_body:
I know this might sound off given the description `shell_plus` being:

> Like the 'shell' command but autoloads the models of all installed Django apps.

However, I'm wondering whether it's possible to bypass model auto-loading altogether. I understand the `SHELL_PLUS_DONT_LOAD` setting allows you to do this on a per-model basis, but my incentive for asking this question is more related to performance.

I currently use the vanilla `shell` command, and legacy IPython's startup features to bootstrap my model and helper methods. I'm not a great fan of this however, as the feature is removed in later versions of IPython, and the same imports are not made available when debugging a production environment.

I just came across the shell plus pre/post imports feature, which would solve our use case nicely. The only thing I'm not a fan of is that the automatic model bootstrapping process takes 5+ seconds, which is a bit of a pain when constantly dropping into a REPL during development. Using the `SHELL_PLUS_DONT_LOAD` option almost certainly wouldn't optimize this, as I imagine a great deal of the time is spend in the [`get_apps_and_models()`](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/management/shells.py#L133) call.

Does it seem reasonable / would you support adding a option to prevent that call being made altogether? Perhaps a `*` value in `SHELL_PLUS_DONT_LOAD` or similar. Alternatively, I could just extend the vanilla shell and try to roll my own, Happy to provide solution and update docs?

issue_title: Wrong Content-Type with a typical browser Accept header
issue_body:
With the following 'Accept' request header :

  `Accept: text/html,*/*`

The response Content-Type is "text/html". It think its wrong, it should be application/json:
- The actual content remains application/json, it is not transformed to valid html
- `*/*` clearly allow the server to return something else than text/html
- Even if application/json is not in Accept (and maybe in the absence of text/javascript), the returned content-type should still be application/json, unless of course the content can be rendered as one of the accepted content types.

issue_title: Update docs: zmq.asyncio.Poller.poll() fails with native sockets
issue_body:
Hello,

We tried creating a zmq.asyncio.Poller object, registering a native socket or fd, and then calling poll(), but doing so raises an exception that the add_recv_event method is not found.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "SimpleServer.py", line 311, in <module>
    ret = loop.run_until_complete(server.arun())
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 387, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 274, in result
    raise self._exception
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "SimpleServer.py", line 189, in arun
    sks = dict(await poller.poll(100))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/zmq/eventloop/future.py", line 65, in poll
    socket._add_recv_event('poll', future=watcher)
AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute '_add_recv_event'

```
 Digging into the code, there is a comment that says the async version of the poller only supports polling on zmq sockets right now.

Recommend updating the docs to make this point clear. Apologies if I missed a note somewhere that warns us against doing this.

Thanks!

With `_AsyncSocket.close` un-awaitable it's possible to stop asyncio event loop before actual cancellation has a chance to execute. At the very least asyncio will show a warning about a pending task.

I see 2 approaches:
- `_AsyncSocket.close` should return a future so that cancellation can be awaited
- `_AsyncSocket` should support `__aenter__` and `__aexit__`

Can I use flake8 tool for linting? I looked in [package.json](https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server/blob/develop/vscode-client/package.json#L22), but did not find there the corresponding options.

if I change `configurationSources` to ['flake'], then I do not see any changes and `pyflakes` continues to be used. I store `flake8` settings in .`flake8` file for all projects.

If this is important, I use [lsp-mode](https://github.com/emacs-lsp/lsp-mode) in Emacs and [LanguageClient-neovim](https://github.com/autozimu/LanguageClient-neovim) in Neovim as clients.

The language-server-protocol defines the `didChangeConfiguration` message to pass configuration data from a client to the language server.

sample server: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/extensions/example-language-server

The protocol specifies a settings object to be sent to the server:

Example:
```
"settings": {
    "python.sys_path": "/opt/sublime_text_3",
    "pydocstyle.line_length": 100,
}
```

Each editor/client may provide its own strategy to present settings to a user, which may be equally designed for each of several supported language-servers. It is hard for such a client to implement strategies to read/write configuration files for each server in different ways, which would also break the idea of a standard interface for providing language-features.

Therefore I'd suggest/request to implement the `didChangeConfiguration` interface in pyls and clearly define and document a set of configurations supported by the server.

This may include the requirement to create an interface for all pyls plugins to be able to extend the set of valid settings and to hook to the configuration object of a workspace.

This request would be the base to accomplish requests like #144.

Another more general reason for this request are configurations which are not yet part of any existing configuration file, such as extending the `PYTHONPATH` / `sys.path` environment variable on a per workspace base. This is required for Jedi to provide completions for custom/local libraries, which are neither part of python's standard search path or the project file tree.

At the moment pyls uses the `sys.path` of the python interpreter it runs on. Some applications such as Sublime Text use their own python interpreter, which uses different search paths and even different standard libraries, which pyls/Jedi provides possibly wrong completions today but at least misses completions for the Sublime Text libraries.

This request is a result of a discussion about possible solutions to extend Jedi's `sys.path` to find Sublime Text libraries at https://github.com/tomv564/LSP/issues/128

issue_title: shared_task defined in custom apps not showing up in registered tasks in periodic tasks admin page
issue_body:
The tasks defined in the `proj/proj/celery.py` file directly are registering successfully.

However, shared tasks defined in the custom django apps are not registering to the database and not showing up in admin at `/admin/django_celery_beat/periodictask/`.

**proj/proj/celery.py**

```
# .. imports.. #
os.environ.setdefault('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE', 'proj.settings')

celery_app = Celery('proj')
celery_app.config_from_object('django.conf:settings', namespace='CELERY')
celery_app.autodiscover_tasks()

@celery_app.task(bind=True)
def hello():
    print "This task is registering successfully"
```

**proj/app/tasks.py**
```
from celery import shared_task

@shared_task
def app_hello():
    print "This task is not registering"
```

issue_title: Incorrect error message when you use a too-short StreamArn/ShortID on a DynamoDB Stream
issue_body:
Example program:

```python
import boto3

client = boto3.client('dynamodbstreams')

shard_iterator = client.get_shard_iterator(
    StreamArn='bumble',
    ShardId='foo',
    ShardIteratorType='LATEST'
)
```

This fails with two errors:

```
botocore.exceptions.ParamValidationError: Parameter validation failed:
Invalid length for parameter StreamArn, value: 6, valid range: 37-inf
Invalid length for parameter ShardId, value: 3, valid range: 28-inf
```

The `-inf` implies that these values can grow without bound; in fact, the stream ARN and shard ID both have upper bounds – I’m not sure about Stream ARN, but the [API docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_streams_GetShardIterator.html) for Shard ID say a max length of 65 chars.

boto3 does enforce the upper limits correctly, but it could provide a better error message when they’re too short.

Env information:

```console
$ python --version
Python 3.6.0

$ python -c 'import boto3; print(boto3.__version__)'
1.4.4
```

How do I pass the xray headers to boto3 so that it may forward them to the services it calls?

There's a particularly sticky point on this: some frameworks (eg Django) make it difficult to get the headers at the time of usage (eg through [watchtower](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/watchtower)). They depend on passing the request object all the way down, making it simply unavailable in many contexts.

In boto there was a `trim_snapshot` method available on the EC2 connection object that assisted in managing EBS snapshot retention.  This doesn't appear to have made it into boto3.  Is this an intentional feature deprecation or is there a replacement planned?

issue_body:
Some of our background tasks generate a lot of different spans (DB, Cache, etc), sometimes more than 10000.
ddtrace has in its internal such code https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/master/ddtrace/writer.py#L233-L235 and it looks like all spans are written to the send queue in one go. Does this mean that:
a) we will see no more than 1000 spans for each trace?
b) it's possible that root span will be dropped from the queue and won't be reported at all?
